shortDescription.text: User-controlled input flows into LLM prompts without sanitization
User-controlled inputs dict flows into workflow variable interpolation without validation
Internal error details or exception descriptions leaked to API consumers
File upload metadata accepted from user without type or size validation at controller layer
Arbitrary key-value dict accepted from user without schema validation
Bare ValueError re-raise may leak internal implementation details to the caller

message.text: The 'query' field from ChatMessagePayload and CompletionMessagePayload is passed directly into AppGenerateService.generate() via the 'args' dict, which feeds it into LLM prompt construction. No sanitization, content filtering, or prompt-injection defenses are applied at the controller layer. An attacker can craft query strings that manipulate the system prompt, extract confidential instructions, or cause the LLM to perform unintended actions.

The 'inputs' dict (arbitrary key-value pairs from the user) in CompletionMessagePayload, ChatMessagePayload, and WorkflowRunPayload is forwarded to AppGenerateService.generate() without content-level sanitization. These inputs are interpolated into prompt templates and workflow variables. A malicious user can inject prompt-manipulation payloads through input variable values to override system instructions or exfiltrate data via the LLM.

WorkflowRunPayload.inputs is a dict[str, Any] that is passed directly into workflow execution. These values are interpolated into workflow node variables (e.g., HTTP request URLs, code execution nodes, database queries) without type checking or allowlist validation at the controller layer. An attacker can inject payloads that are interpreted by downstream workflow nodes, potentially achieving SSRF, code execution, or data exfiltration depending on the workflow configuration.

The 'files' parameter in CompletionMessagePayload, ChatMessagePayload, and WorkflowRunPayload accepts list[dict[str, Any]] with no schema validation on file type, size, content-type, or allowed extensions at the controller layer. Malicious file metadata could be used to trigger unexpected behavior in downstream file processing services (e.g., SSRF via URL-type files, path traversal via filenames, or resource exhaustion via large uploads).

The 'inputs' field is typed as dict[str, Any] in all three payload models (CompletionMessagePayload, ChatMessagePayload, WorkflowRunPayload). There is no allowlist of expected keys, no type constraints on values, and no length limits. This allows an attacker to pass arbitrary data (deeply nested objects, very large strings, unexpected types) which may cause downstream issues including denial of service, type confusion in variable interpolation, or injection attacks in template rendering.

Multiple exception handlers forward ex.description directly to the HTTP response (e.g., ProviderNotInitializeError(ex.description), CompletionRequestError(e.description)). These descriptions may contain internal provider names, model identifiers, rate limit details, or stack information that aids an attacker in reconnaissance. The bare 'raise e' for ValueError also leaks internal validation messages.

ValueError exceptions are re-raised directly ('raise e') without wrapping, exposing internal validation error messages to end users. In completion.py this pattern appears in both CompletionApi.post and ChatApi.post. These messages may reveal internal field names, expected formats, or configuration details useful for targeted attacks.
